summary: The chorion is formed by the trophoblast and extraembryonic somatic mesoderm.
The chorion is formed by the epiblast and intraembryonic mesoderm.
The chorion is formed by the amnion and yolk sac membrane.
Chorion frondosum faces the decidua basalis and has proliferating villi that form the fetal part of the placenta.
Chorion frondosum is the smooth part of the chorion facing the decidua capsularis.
Chorion frondosum is the part of the amnion that produces amniotic fluid.
After 16 weeks, the main source of amniotic fluid is fetal urine, and the fetus removes fluid by swallowing.
After 16 weeks, amniotic fluid is produced entirely by the amnion epithelium.
Amniotic fluid is derived primarily from maternal blood plasma throughout pregnancy.
The human placenta is haemochorial, meaning maternal blood directly bathes the chorionic villi.
The human placenta is epitheliochorial, with six tissue layers separating maternal and fetal blood.
The human placenta is endotheliochorial, with maternal endothelium in contact with chorionic villi.
Potter sequence results from oligohydramnios caused by bilateral renal agenesis.
Potter sequence results from polyhydramnios caused by oesophageal atresia.
Potter sequence is caused by excess amniotic fluid compressing the fetal lungs.
The umbilical cord normally contains two arteries and one vein surrounded by Wharton's jelly.
The umbilical cord normally contains one artery and two veins.
The umbilical cord contains two arteries and two veins.
Polyhydramnios is defined as amniotic fluid volume exceeding 2 litres and may be associated with oesophageal atresia or anencephaly.
Polyhydramnios is defined as amniotic fluid less than 500 mL and is associated with renal agenesis.
Polyhydramnios is a normal finding in twin pregnancies and requires no investigation.
